title: An Introduction to the Scott Complexity of Countable Structures and a Survey of Recent Results
overall_summary: This survey explains Scott sentences, Scott rank, and Scott complexity of countable structures via infinitary logic L_{ω1ω}, back-and-forth relations, orbit definability, and Wadge/descriptive-set-theoretic complexity of isomorphism classes. The most directly relevant material for the original problem is the equivalence, due to Montalbán, between low Scott complexity and low infinitary definability of automorphism orbits, together with the parameter-naming theorems showing exactly how finite parameters can lower Scott complexity. The paper also records effective Nadel-style back-and-forth theorems: for X-computable structures, agreement up to all X-computable infinitary formulas forces isomorphism or automorphism, and low Scott rank is equivalent to having an X-computable Scott sentence. Finally, the survey includes the Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman/Chisholm characterization of relative computable categoricity by computable Σ1 Scott families, plus Turetsky examples separating computable categoricity from hyperarithmetic/Scott-rank tameness; these are useful reference points when comparing computability of automorphisms with Σ1-in-definability from finite parameters.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2 (Scott; canonical Scott sentence)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable first-order structure. For finite tuples ā and b̄ from A, let ā ≡∞ b̄ mean that ā and b̄ are equivalent in all standard back-and-forth relations, equivalently that they satisfy the same L_{ω1ω} formulas in A. For each tuple ā, one can choose an L_{ω1ω} formula ψ_ā(x̄), including all atomic facts true of ā, such that A ⊨ ψ_ā(b̄) if and only if ā ≡∞ b̄. Define extension axioms ρ_∅ := ∀x ∨_{b∈A} ψ_b(x), ρ_ā := ∀ū(ψ_ā(ū) → ∀x ∨_{b∈A} ψ_{āb}(ū,x)), σ_∅ := ∧_{b∈A} ∃x ψ_b(x), and σ_ā := ∀ū(ψ_ā(ū) → ∧_{b∈A} ∃x ψ_{āb}(ū,x)). Then θ := ∧_{ā∈A} (ρ_ā ∧ σ_ā), with the empty tuple case included, is a Scott sentence for A. In particular, every countable structure has an L_{ω1ω} sentence whose countable models are exactly the copies of A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each pair of tuples ā,b̄ with ā not ≡∞ b̄, choose an L_{ω1ω} formula φ_{ā;b̄}(x̄) true of ā and false of b̄. Let ψ_ā be the conjunction of all such separating formulas, over b̄ outside the ≡∞-class of ā, together with the atomic diagram of ā. Thus ψ_ā defines the ≡∞-class of ā inside A. The sentence θ asserts both forth and back extension properties for these ψ-types. If B is a countable model of θ, maintain a finite partial map from A to B sending ā to b̄ with B ⊨ ψ_ā(b̄). The atomic conjuncts in ψ_ā make this map a finite partial isomorphism. Given a new element a′ of A, the σ_ā axiom produces b′ in B with B ⊨ ψ_{āa′}(b̄,b′). Given a new element b′ of B, the ρ_ā axiom produces a′ in A with B ⊨ ψ_{āa′}(b̄,b′). A standard back-and-forth construction over enumerations of A and B yields an isomorphism A ≅ B.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.3 (automorphisms and ≡∞)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let ā,b̄ be finite tuples from A of the same length. There is an automorphism of A taking ā to b̄ if and only if ā ≡∞ b̄, i.e. if and only if ā and b̄ are equivalent in every back-and-forth relation or, equivalently, satisfy the same L_{ω1ω} formulas.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward direction is invariance of L_{ω1ω} formulas under automorphisms. For the reverse direction, expand the language by constants naming the coordinates of the tuple. If ā ≡∞ b̄, then the expanded pointed structures (A,ā) and (A,b̄) satisfy the same infinitary formulas; applying the Scott-sentence/back-and-forth argument to the expanded structures yields an isomorphism (A,ā) ≅ (A,b̄), which is exactly an automorphism of A sending ā to b̄.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Orbit-definability estimate after Definition 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let ≤_α be the asymmetric back-and-forth relations. Suppose α is a countable ordinal such that for all finite tuples ā,b̄ from A, if ā ≤_α b̄ then ā ≡∞ b̄; equivalently, every tuple has stabilized by level α. Then each automorphism orbit of a finite tuple ā is definable in A by a Π_α formula, and the canonical Scott sentence constructed from these orbit formulas can be taken to have complexity Π_{α+2}. More generally, if r(ā) is the least ordinal such that ā ≤_{r(ā)} b̄ implies ā ≡∞ b̄ for all b̄, then the orbit of ā is definable by a Π_{r(ā)} formula.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For b̄ outside the automorphism orbit of ā, one has ā not ≡∞ b̄, hence by the assumed bound ā not ≤_α b̄. By the characterization of ≤_α in terms of Σ_α formulas, ā not ≤_α b̄ yields a Σ_α formula true of b̄ but false of ā; negating it gives a Π_α formula true of ā and false of b̄. Conjoining these Π_α separators over the countably many b̄ outside the orbit defines exactly the automorphism orbit of ā. Substituting these Π_α orbit formulas into the canonical Scott-sentence construction raises the complexity by two quantifier alternation levels, giving a Π_{α+2} Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.8 (Montalbán; robust Scott rank)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let α be a countable ordinal. The following are equivalent: (1) A has a Π_{α+1} Scott sentence; (2) every automorphism orbit of every finite tuple in A is Σ_α-definable without parameters; (3) A is uniformly boldface Δ^0_α-categorical, in the standard sense that isomorphisms between copies of A are uniformly obtainable at complexity Δ^0_α relative to the presentations/copies involved.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey states this theorem as a result of Montalbán and does not give a full proof. The mechanism indicated by the surrounding discussion is as follows. Low-complexity Scott sentences encode enough back-and-forth information to isolate tuple orbits by Σ_α formulas. Conversely, if all tuple-orbits are Σ_α-definable, one can use those orbit definitions in the canonical Scott-sentence back-and-forth axioms to obtain a Π_{α+1} Scott sentence. The equivalence with uniform Δ^0_α-categoricity comes from using the Σ_α orbit definitions to build isomorphisms between copies by choosing images of tuples in their corresponding orbits, and conversely extracting orbit definitions from uniformly low-complexity isomorphism procedures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1 (Montalbán; naming parameters and Σ Scott sentences)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let α be a countable ordinal. Then A has a Σ_{α+1} Scott sentence if and only if there is a finite tuple c̄ from A such that the expanded structure (A,c̄), obtained by naming c̄ as constants, has a Π_α Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey emphasizes that this fact is less immediate than it may appear and cites Montalbán and Alvir-Greenberg-Harrison-Trainor-Turetsky for proofs; no full proof is included. The easy direction is that if (A,c̄) has a Π_α Scott sentence θ(c̄), then ∃x̄ θ(x̄), with the constants replaced by variables, is a Σ_{α+1} Scott sentence for A. The converse requires showing that from a Σ_{α+1} Scott description of A one can choose a finite tuple of witnesses in A whose naming converts the description into a Π_α Scott sentence for the pointed structure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2 (parameterized robust Scott rank)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let α be a countable ordinal. The following are equivalent: (1) A has a Σ_{α+2} Scott sentence; (2) there is a finite tuple c̄ from A such that every automorphism orbit of every finite tuple in A is Σ_α-definable over the parameters c̄; (3) A is boldface Δ^0_α-categorical, in the non-uniform parameterized sense used in computable structure theory.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is obtained in the survey by combining Theorem 2.8 with Theorem 3.1. Namely, A has a Σ_{α+2} Scott sentence iff, by Theorem 3.1 with α+1 in place of α, some finite expansion (A,c̄) has a Π_{α+1} Scott sentence. Applying Theorem 2.8 to the expanded structure says that the automorphism orbits in (A,c̄), equivalently the orbits in A over c̄, are Σ_α-definable, and gives the corresponding Δ^0_α-categoricity characterization.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.16 (Alvir-Greenberg-Harrison-Trainor-Turetsky; d-Σ_α and parameters)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let α be a countable ordinal. Then A has a d-Σ_α Scott sentence, meaning a Scott sentence that is a conjunction of a Σ_α sentence and a Π_α sentence, if and only if there is a finite tuple c̄ from A such that (A,c̄) has a Π_α Scott sentence and the automorphism orbit of c̄ in A is Σ_α-definable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey records this theorem from Alvir-Greenberg-Harrison-Trainor-Turetsky and does not include the full proof. The stated criterion separates two tasks: after naming a suitable finite tuple c̄, the pointed structure must have a Π_α Scott description, and in the unpointed structure the possible images of c̄ must be recognizable by a Σ_α formula. These two pieces combine to describe exactly the copies of A by a difference-of-Σ_α sentence; conversely, from a d-Σ_α Scott sentence one can extract such a tuple and orbit definition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.3 (A. Miller; collapse from both Σ and Π to d-Σ)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let α be a countable ordinal. If A has both a Σ_α Scott sentence and a Π_α Scott sentence, then A has a d-Σ_β Scott sentence for some β < α.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey cites this theorem of A. Miller and gives no proof. It is used to justify that, when measuring least Scott-sentence complexity, one must include d-Σ classes: if a structure is describable at both sides of a level, it is describable as a difference at some lower level.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.5 (Lopez-Escobar, Vaught, D. Miller)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a countable language and let Mod(L) be the Polish space of L-structures with domain ω. Let K ⊆ Mod(L) be closed under isomorphism. For every countable ordinal α, K is a Borel Σ^0_α set if and only if K is axiomatized by an infinitary Σ_α sentence of L_{ω1ω}. The analogous equivalences hold for Π^0_α and Π_α, and for the corresponding levels of the difference hierarchy.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey states this as the Lopez-Escobar theorem together with strengthenings by Vaught and D. Miller. The result supplies the bridge between syntactic Scott-sentence complexity and descriptive-set-theoretic complexity of isomorphism-invariant classes of countable structures. No proof is given in the survey.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.10 (possible Scott complexities)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For countable structures, the possible Scott complexities, viewed as Wadge degrees of isomorphism classes Iso(A) in Mod(L), are exactly: Π^0_α for every α ≥ 1; Σ^0_α for every successor ordinal α ≥ 3; and d-Σ^0_α for every successor ordinal α ≥ 1. Moreover, for each of these Wadge degrees there is a countable structure whose isomorphism class has exactly that degree.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey attributes the result to A. Miller and to Alvir-Greenberg-Harrison-Trainor-Turetsky. The surrounding discussion explains the strategy: use Wadge reducibility and the Lopez-Escobar correspondence to replace arbitrary-looking syntactic complexity classes by Wadge degrees of isomorphism classes; prove that other natural difference-hierarchy candidates collapse to one of Σ, Π, or d-Σ for Scott sentences; and construct examples realizing each listed degree. Full constructions and exclusions are not reproduced in the survey.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1 (rank of a computable well-founded tree)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ⊆ ω. If T ⊆ ω^{<ω} is an X-computable well-founded tree, and tree rank is defined by rk(s)=0 for leaves and rk(s) equal to the least ordinal greater than the ranks of all children of s otherwise, then the rank of T is an X-computable ordinal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given T, define the Kleene-Brouwer order ≤_{KB} on nodes of T by s ≤_{KB} t if either t is an initial segment of s, or at the first coordinate where s and t differ, s has the smaller value. If T is well-founded, then the Kleene-Brouwer order is a well-order computable from T. Its order type is an X-computable ordinal and bounds the tree rank of T from above. Since initial segments of X-computable well-orders have X-computable presentations, the actual tree rank is also an X-computable ordinal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.3 (Nadel; effective back-and-forth isomorphism theorem)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ⊆ ω and let A and B be X-computable countable structures. Extend the asymmetric back-and-forth relations ≤_α and ≡_α to tuples from possibly different structures. If A ≡_α B, for the empty tuples, for every α < ω^X_1, where ω^X_1 is the least non-X-computable ordinal, then A and B are isomorphic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Build an X-computable tree T of finite partial isomorphisms from A to B. At level n require that the partial isomorphism contain the first n elements of A in its domain and the first n elements of B in its range. A path through T is a total isomorphism. The key claim is that if a node σ at some level maps ā to b̄ and (A,ā) ≡_{α⋅4} (B,b̄), then the tree rank of σ is at least α. For β < α, first add the next required element d of B; using back-and-forth equivalence obtain c in A with (A,āc) ≡_{β⋅4+2} (B,b̄d). Then add the next required element c′ of A; use the remaining equivalence to obtain d′ in B with (A,ācc′) ≡_{β⋅4} (B,b̄dd′). This gives a child of σ of rank at least β by induction. Since the empty tuples are equivalent at all α < ω^X_1, the rank of T would be at least ω^X_1 if T were well-founded, contradicting Theorem 4.1 because T is X-computable. Hence T has a path, yielding an isomorphism A ≅ B.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.4 (Nadel; effective automorphism criterion for tuples)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ⊆ ω, let A be an X-computable structure, and let ā,b̄ be finite tuples from A. If ā ≡_α b̄ for every α < ω^X_1, then there is an automorphism of A taking ā to b̄.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 4.3 to the two expanded X-computable structures (A,ā) and (A,b̄), where the entries of the tuples are named by constants. The hypothesis says that these pointed structures are equivalent in all back-and-forth relations below ω^X_1, so they are isomorphic. The resulting isomorphism is an automorphism of A sending ā to b̄.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.5 (Nadel; upper bound on Scott complexity of X-computable structures)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ⊆ ω. Every X-computable structure has a Π_{ω^X_1+2} Scott sentence, and hence has Scott rank at most ω^X_1+1 in the sense of the Scott-rank notions discussed in the survey.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Corollary 4.4, if two tuples in an X-computable structure are equivalent in all back-and-forth relations below ω^X_1, then they are in the same automorphism orbit. Therefore the back-and-forth analysis has stabilized by level ω^X_1. In the canonical Scott-sentence construction, one can use formulas at complexity Π_{ω^X_1} to define tuple orbits, and the extension axioms then give a Π_{ω^X_1+2} Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.7 (Nadel; computable Scott sentence iff rank below ω^X_1)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ⊆ ω and let A be an X-computable countable structure. Then A has Scott rank strictly less than ω^X_1 if and only if A has an X-computable Scott sentence, meaning a Scott sentence in computable infinitary logic relative to X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each α < ω^X_1 and tuple ā in A, define computable infinitary formulas φ_{ā,α} and ψ_{ā,α}. The formula φ_{ā,α}(x̄) is true in a structure B of b̄ exactly when (A,ā) ≤_α (B,b̄); the formula ψ_{ā,α}(x̄) is true exactly when (B,b̄) ≤_α (A,ā). At α=0 both are the conjunction of the first |x̄|-many atomic and negated atomic formulas true of ā. For α>0, set φ_{ā,α}(x̄) := ∧_{β<α} ∀ȳ ∨_{c̄∈A} ψ_{āc̄,β}(x̄,ȳ), and ψ_{ā,α}(x̄) := ∧_{β<α} ∧_{c̄∈A} ∃ȳ φ_{āc̄,β}(x̄,ȳ). These are X-computable Π_{α⋅2} formulas. If some X-computable ordinal α bounds the stabilization of tuple orbits in A, then using ψ_{ā,α} as the orbit formula in the canonical Scott sentence gives an X-computable Π_{α⋅2+2} Scott sentence. Conversely, any X-computable Scott sentence has ordinal complexity below ω^X_1, forcing the Scott rank to be below ω^X_1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.8 and Corollary 4.9 (Nadel; computable infinitary theories determine isomorphism and automorphism)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X ⊆ ω. If A and B are X-computable structures satisfying the same X-computable infinitary L_{ω1ω} sentences, then A and B are isomorphic. Likewise, if A is X-computable and tuples ā,b̄ from A satisfy the same X-computable infinitary formulas, then there is an automorphism of A taking ā to b̄.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each α < ω^X_1, use the formulas φ_{∅,α} constructed in the proof of Theorem 4.7. If A and B satisfy the same X-computable infinitary sentences, then B satisfies the formula expressing A ≤_α B for every α, and symmetrically A ≤_α B and B ≤_α A for all α < ω^X_1. Theorem 4.3 then gives A ≅ B. The tuple version is obtained by naming the tuples as constants and applying the same argument to the expanded structures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.10 (Harrison linear order, relativized)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every X ∈ 2^ω, there is an X-computable linear order of order type ω^X_1 ⋅ (1+Q), where Q is the order type of the rationals. This order has Scott rank ω^X_1+1 and Scott complexity Π_{ω^X_1+2}. Moreover, there is a uniform operator Φ such that Φ(X) has order type ω^X_1 ⋅ (1+Q); hence ω^X_1 = ω^Y_1 if and only if Φ(X) ≅ Φ(Y).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey gives a constructive account. The relation saying that Y is not hyperarithmetic in X is Σ^1_1(X), so there is an X-computable tree T whose paths are pairs ⟨Y,f⟩ witnessing non-hyperarithmeticity; T has no X-hyperarithmetic path. The Kleene-Brouwer order L of T is X-computable and has no X-hyperarithmetic descending sequence. Analyze the well-founded initial segment of L: its order type cannot be X-computable, else one could split off the ill-founded tail hyperarithmetically and compute a descending sequence; it also cannot exceed ω^X_1, so it is exactly ω^X_1. Repeating this analysis above any point shows L has order type ω^X_1⋅(1+L*)+α for some α<ω^X_1. The tail α is X-computable, and L* is dense without endpoints; replacing L by L⋅ω removes α and yields ω^X_1⋅(1+Q). For the Scott-complexity lower bound, take an element u in the non-well-founded part. If its automorphism orbit were definable by a Σ_α formula for α<ω^X_1, the effective formulas of Theorem 4.7 allow one, after increasing α, to assume an X-computable definition. That would make the orbit X-hyperarithmetic; since this orbit has no least element, it would compute an X-hyperarithmetic descending sequence in L, contradiction. Therefore the complexity is Π_{ω^X_1+2}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.11 (Knight-Millar; computable structures of rank ω^X_1)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every X ⊆ ω, there is an X-computable structure of Scott rank ω^X_1 and Scott complexity Π_{ω^X_1}. The survey presents this via X-computable thin homogeneous trees. A tree is thin if, at each level, the ordinal ranks of nodes of ordinal rank are bounded by an X-computable ordinal. A tree is homogeneous if whenever a node has a successor of rank α it has infinitely many such successors, and if some node at level n has a successor of rank α then every node at level n with rank greater than α has a successor of rank α.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof sketch in the survey replaces Barwise compactness with a construction from the Harrison order H. Choose H so that successors, predecessors, and limit elements are computable. Build sets S_1⊆S_2⊆...⊆H such that their union is H, S_1 is cofinal, each S_n is bounded above in the well-founded part of H, limits in S_n are approached from below by elements of S_{n+1}, and predecessors of successor elements of S_n are placed in S_{n+1}. Let T be the subtree of descending sequences ⟨h_1,...,h_n⟩ in H with h_i∈S_i. The rank of a node is the order type of the segment below its last element if that last element lies in the well-founded part of H, and is ∞ otherwise. The boundedness of S_n makes the tree thin at each level, while the union of the S_n makes the ranks unbounded below ω^X_1 overall. Duplicating nodes infinitely many times enforces homogeneity. A computable thin homogeneous tree has a Π_{ω^X_1} Scott sentence because one can describe, level by level, exactly which ranks occur; unboundedness of the ranks gives the lower bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.25 (Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman; Chisholm; relative computable categoricity and Σ1 Scott families)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure. A is relatively computably categorical, meaning that for every copy B of A there is an isomorphism A → B computable from the atomic diagram of B, if and only if A has a computable Σ1 Scott family. In the formulation in the survey, this means that there is a finite tuple c̄ of constants/parameters and a computably enumerable collection Φ of computable Σ1 formulas such that: (1) every finite tuple ā in A satisfies φ(ā,c̄) for some φ ∈ Φ; and (2) whenever two tuples ā,b̄ in A satisfy the same formula φ(·,c̄) from Φ, there is an automorphism of A taking ā to b̄.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey notes that the direction from a computable Σ1 Scott family to relative computable categoricity is straightforward: the formulas in Φ give effectively recognizable orbit labels for finite tuples over the finite parameter tuple, and a back-and-forth construction can build a B-computable isomorphism to any copy by matching tuples satisfying the same Scott-family formula. The converse is described as a forcing argument: from the hypothesis that every copy has a copy-computable isomorphism back to A, one forces/generically analyzes finite partial isomorphisms to extract a computably enumerable family of existential formulas that covers tuples and refines them exactly up to automorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Discussion before Theorem 4.26 (relative Δ^1_1-categoricity and computable Scott rank)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a computable structure A, if A has computable Scott rank α < ω^CK_1, then A is relatively Δ^0_{α⋅2}-categorical. Conversely, a computable structure that is relatively Δ^1_1-categorical must have computable Scott rank. Thus, as stated in the survey, a computable structure is relatively Δ^1_1-categorical if and only if it has computable Scott rank.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward implication follows from the same effective back-and-forth analysis as Theorem 4.8: computable orbit approximations below a computable ordinal give a hyperarithmetical procedure for constructing isomorphisms between copies. The converse is attributed in the survey to an argument referenced as Proposition 1 of Turetsky: if isomorphisms to all copies were uniformly hyperarithmetical relative to the copies, then the structure could not have high Scott rank.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.26 (Turetsky; computably categorical but high Scott complexity)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There exists a computably categorical computable structure with high Scott complexity. Consequently, this structure is not relatively Δ^1_1-categorical.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey states this theorem and does not reproduce Turetsky's construction. Its role is to show that ordinary computable categoricity can coexist with high Scott complexity, so having computable isomorphisms between computable copies need not give low infinitary orbit definitions or relative hyperarithmetic categoricity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.10 (Knight-Saraph; finitely generated structures have Σ3 Scott sentences)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every finitely generated countable structure, in any first-order language, has a Σ3 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey records this theorem and notes that the proof works for arbitrary languages. The standard idea is to choose a finite generating tuple ḡ. A Σ3 sentence can assert the existence of a tuple x̄ with the complete atomic behavior of ḡ, together with the assertion that every element of the structure is denoted by some term in x̄. The complete term diagram fixes the quotient of the term algebra generated by x̄, and the generation clause ensures that no extra elements are present. The survey does not give full details.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.15 (characterizations of d-Σ2 Scott sentences for finitely generated structures)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a finitely generated structure. The following are equivalent: (1) A has a d-Σ2 Scott sentence; (2) A has a Π3 Scott sentence; (3) A is the only model of its Σ2 theory; (4) some generating tuple of A is defined by a Π1 formula; (5) every generating tuple of A is defined by a Π1 formula; (6) A does not contain a copy of itself as a proper Σ1-elementary substructure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey attributes the equivalences to A. Miller, Harrison-Trainor-Ho, and Alvir-Knight-McCoy and does not include the full proof. It highlights condition (6) as especially useful for examples because it is semantic: failure of a d-Σ2 Scott sentence for a finitely generated structure is witnessed by a proper self-copy that is Σ1-elementary. Conditions (4) and (5) connect the low Scott complexity to Π1 definability of generating tuples.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.16 (Alvir-Knight-McCoy; computable d-Σ2 for finitely generated structures)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a finitely generated computable structure. The following are equivalent: (1) A has a computable d-Σ2 Scott sentence; (2) some generating tuple of A is defined by a computable Π1 formula; (3) every generating tuple of A is defined by a computable Π1 formula.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The survey cites this theorem and gives no proof. It is the effective analogue of the generating-tuple clauses in Theorem 5.15: computable d-Σ2 descriptions of finitely generated structures correspond exactly to computable Π1 definitions of their generating tuples.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical Scott sentence from orbit formulas
proof_techniques.description: The paper's foundational construction first defines, for each finite tuple ā of a countable structure A, an infinitary formula ψ_ā isolating the ≡∞-class of ā. These formulas are then used in extension axioms saying that every partial match of a tuple can be extended both forth and back. The resulting sentence is a Scott sentence because any countable model satisfying the same extension axioms supports a full back-and-forth isomorphism with A.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original problem, this gives a template for turning information about tuple orbits over finite parameters into definitions of automorphism graphs. In particular, if orbits of tuples containing a, π(a), x, and y are low-existentially definable, then the graph of π can often be expressed as a countable union of such orbit conditions.

proof_techniques.name: Back-and-forth rank bounds give orbit-definability bounds
proof_techniques.description: The asymmetric relations ≤_α are calibrated so that failure of ā ≤_α b̄ is witnessed by a Π_α formula true of ā and false of b̄. Once the back-and-forth analysis stabilizes at α, the automorphism orbit of any tuple is the conjunction of Π_α separators against all tuples outside the orbit. This produces both orbit definitions and Scott sentences of controlled complexity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A route to proving non-Σ1-in-definability of some automorphism is to force, over any chosen finite parameter set, some relevant tuple-orbit information not to stabilize at the existential level. Conversely, any attempt to prove that all automorphisms are Σ1-in-definable from finite data may be reducible to proving low-rank orbit stabilization over those parameters.

proof_techniques.name: Parameter shift between Σ Scott sentences and pointed Π Scott sentences
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3.1 and Theorem 3.2 explain how finite parameters affect Scott complexity: a Σ_{α+2} Scott sentence for A is equivalent to the existence of finite parameters c̄ such that all automorphism orbits are Σ_α-definable over c̄. Theorem 4.16 gives the analogous d-Σ_α criterion: name a tuple c̄, require a Π_α Scott sentence for the pointed structure, and require the orbit of c̄ itself to be Σ_α-definable in the original structure.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem explicitly allows parameters ā∪π(ā). These theorems suggest analyzing each finite parameter expansion (A,p̄): if some finite expansion has all tuple orbits Σ1-definable, then many automorphism graphs may become Σ1-in-definable; a candidate example should avoid such a uniform finite-parameter collapse.

proof_techniques.name: Effective tree of finite partial isomorphisms
proof_techniques.description: Nadel's proof builds an X-computable tree whose nodes are finite partial isomorphisms satisfying increasing domain and range requirements. If sufficiently high back-and-forth equivalence holds, each node has large ordinal tree rank. Since an X-computable well-founded tree has X-computable rank, a rank bound reaching ω^X_1 forces the tree to be ill-founded, and a path gives an isomorphism.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This method can be adapted to automorphisms by working in a single structure with constants for a tuple and its proposed image. It is a possible way to convert infinitary equivalence or orbit information into actual automorphisms while tracking what is computable from a representation plus a cone oracle.

proof_techniques.name: Computable infinitary approximants φ_{ā,α} and ψ_{ā,α}
proof_techniques.description: For an X-computable structure A, the survey defines formulas φ_{ā,α} and ψ_{ā,α} that express one-sided back-and-forth reducibility between (A,ā) and an arbitrary tuple in another structure. These formulas are X-computable Π_{α⋅2} formulas and give a normal form for effective back-and-forth analysis. If a computable ordinal α bounds orbit stabilization, substituting these formulas into the canonical construction yields an X-computable Scott sentence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The α=1 and low-α cases are especially relevant to Σ1-in-definability questions. They offer a standard normal form for comparing existential infinitary definitions with computable procedures relative to the atomic diagram of a copy.

proof_techniques.name: Scott families as effective orbit partitions
proof_techniques.description: A computable Σ1 Scott family is a computably enumerable set of existential computable infinitary formulas, possibly over finitely many constants, that covers all tuples and refines them exactly up to automorphism. The Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman/Chisholm theorem says this is exactly the structural content of relative computable categoricity. The easy direction uses the Scott family to build isomorphisms by matching finite tuples with the same formula.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original property concerns all automorphisms being computable relative to a copy and cone oracle rather than existence of one computable isomorphism, Scott families are the closest standard tool for relating computability of maps to Σ1 orbit information. If a candidate structure accidentally has finite-parameter Σ1 Scott families, the desired nondefinability property may fail.

proof_techniques.name: High Scott rank from Harrison pseudo-well-orderings
proof_techniques.description: The Harrison-order construction uses an X-computable linear order with no X-hyperarithmetic descending sequence. Low-complexity definability of an orbit in its non-well-founded part would make that orbit hyperarithmetic and, because it has no least element, would yield a forbidden hyperarithmetic descending sequence. This converts absence of hyperarithmetic descending paths into lower bounds on Scott complexity and orbit definability.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a reusable obstruction pattern: arrange that a low existential definition of an automorphism graph or orbit would compute a forbidden descending sequence or path. Such components might be combined with coding that keeps automorphisms computable on a cone.

proof_techniques.name: Thin homogeneous trees to control ranks level-by-level
proof_techniques.description: The Knight-Millar construction builds a computable tree whose ordinal ranks are bounded at each finite level but unbounded overall below ω^CK_1. Homogeneity ensures that rank data determines orbit behavior, while thinness gives an upper-bound Scott sentence. Duplicating nodes infinitely often creates enough symmetry without changing the intended rank spectrum.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Homogeneous trees provide a flexible way to create many automorphisms whose behavior is governed by rank labels. They may be useful as components where finite parameter sets see only bounded local rank information, while globally some automorphism behavior remains beyond Σ1-in-definability.

proof_techniques.name: Finitely generated d-Σ2 criteria via self-embeddings
proof_techniques.description: For finitely generated structures, the survey records sharp criteria for having d-Σ2 Scott sentences: equivalently, generating tuples are Π1-definable, or there is no proper Σ1-elementary self-copy. The effective version says computable d-Σ2 Scott sentences are exactly computable Π1 definitions of generating tuples.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests caution with finitely generated candidates for the original problem: once a generating tuple and its image are named, an automorphism is often term-determined and may become low-definable. The self-embedding criterion can help diagnose when finite generation forces low parameterized complexity.

proof_techniques.name: Descriptive-set-theoretic lower bounds via Wadge reductions
proof_techniques.description: The survey explains that Scott complexity can be measured by the Wadge degree of Iso(A), the set of copies of A in the Polish space of countable structures. Lopez-Escobar converts Borel complexity of isomorphism-invariant classes into infinitary sentence complexity, and Wadge reductions from complete sets prove optimality of Scott-sentence complexity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If non-Σ1-in-definability of automorphisms can be reframed as nonexistence of a low-complexity invariant relation on copies or pointed copies, Wadge-style reductions may provide robust lower bounds independent of a particular presentation.

other_useful_info: The survey explicitly distinguishes parameterless Scott rank from parameterized Scott rank: using Σ-style Scott rank has the feature that naming finitely many constants does not change the rank in the same way, and Theorems 3.1 and 3.2 are the formal bridge. Around Theorem 4.16, the survey notes that certain high Scott complexity cases are exactly structures of Scott rank ω^CK_1+1 whose rank drops to ω^CK_1 after naming a constant; these are examples where finite parameters materially change Scott complexity. The Turetsky result that a computably categorical structure can have high Scott complexity is a useful warning that ordinary computable categoricity does not imply low orbit definability or relative Δ^1_1-categoricity. Key cited sources for deeper proofs are Montalbán [Mon15, Mon17] for robust Scott rank and parameterized Scott rank, Alvir-Greenberg-Harrison-Trainor-Turetsky [AGHTT] for Scott complexity and d-Σ parameter criteria, Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman/Chisholm for Σ1 Scott families and relative computable categoricity, Nadel [Nad74] for admissible/effective Scott-sentence bounds, and Turetsky [Tur] for computably categorical high-rank examples.
why_relevant: A concise guide to Scott complexity, orbit-definability, and recent constructions. It is useful for locating normal-form arguments and known examples where finite parameters change, or fail to change, Scott complexity.
